title: Sudden Vision Loss in Dogs: Why Your Dog Is Bumping into Furniture
meta_desc: If your dog is suddenly running into furniture, it may be a medical emergency. Learn about acute blindness and the urgency of veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden bumping into furniture is a high-priority veterinary emergency indicating acute blindness or neurological issues. Immediate care is essential to prevent permanent vision loss and manage pain.

What causes a dog to suddenly start bumping into furniture or walls?

  • Indicates a rapid loss of vision (acute blindness) or severe neurological impairment.
  • This is a sudden change in environmental perception, not a normal sign of aging.
  • Potential causes include Sudden Acquired Retinal Degeneration Syndrome (SARDs), acute glaucoma, retinal detachment, stroke, or brain inflammation.

Is it an emergency if my dog suddenly loses their vision or seems disoriented?

  • The urgency level is High; this is considered a medical emergency.
  • Immediate treatment is often the only way to prevent permanent blindness or manage intense ocular pain.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your dog is distressed, has visible eye changes, or is pressing their head against surfaces.

How can photos or videos help a veterinarian triage my dog's sudden vision loss?

  • Clear, well-lit photos can show if pupils are dilated, cloudy, red, or inflamed.
  • Short videos of your dog walking help determine if the issue is visual or relates to a lack of coordination (ataxia).
  • Visual evidence helps narrow down potential causes before you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Sudden vision loss in dogs can stem from various causes, including sudden acquired retinal degeneration syndrome (SARDS), optic neuritis, retinal detachment, and intracranial disease affecting the optic chiasm, tracts, or occipital cortex. SARDS presents with acute blindness, dilated pupils with diminished or absent pupillary light reflexes, and a normal-appearing fundus, often affecting middle-aged dogs who may also exhibit signs of liver disease or hyperadrenocorticism. Optic neuritis manifests as acute blindness, potentially affecting both eyes, and may be associated with systemic diseases such as canine distemper or fungal infections, with treatment involving systemic corticosteroids. Retinal detachment can also cause acute blindness accompanied by hemorrhage. Intracranial disease causes acute blindness with normal pupillary light reflexes.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: Could my dog just be acting confused?
faqs.answer: While confusion can happen, sudden physical bumping into objects is almost always a sign of a significant underlying medical issue that requires professional diagnosis.

faqs.question: What should I do to keep my dog safe right now?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog in a small, carpeted area or a crate to prevent them from falling down stairs or hitting sharp corners until you can reach a veterinarian.

faqs.question: Can sudden blindness in dogs be reversed?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends entirely on the cause. Some conditions like high blood pressure or certain infections can be treated to restore sight, while others may be permanent.
